coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of photoluminescent carbon dots derived from red beetroot as a selective probe for Pd2+ detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Hashemi, Nazanin
Mousazadeh, Mohammad H.
coredata.dc:description: Recently, the development of green methods for carbon dots (CDs) synthesis using natural precursors has received considerable attention. In this regard, fluorescent CDs with high quantum yield have been synthesized from Red Beetroot as a natural precursor via a low-cost, simple, and green one-step hydrothermal treatment. The prepared CDs with a size range of 5–7 nm exhibited significant advantages, including great water solubility, low cytotoxicity, high quantum yield (27.6%), incredible resistance to pH alteration, high salty environments, and photobleaching. The fluorescence emission wavelength with maximum intensity for CDs was 525 nm at the excitation wavelength of 450 nm. These green-emitting CDs were employed for selective and sensitive measurement of Pd2+ in an aqueous solution. The addition of Pd2+ resulted in fluorescence quenching of CDs through a static quenching mechanism with an acceptable limit of detection of 33 nM in a linear range from 3 μM to 43 μM. Also, the proposed fluorescent probe can be applied as a suitable sensor for measuring Pd2+ ions in Mineral and Well water samples with satisfactory recovery rates from 96.6 to 105.0%. Generated fluorescent CDs have been used as an attractive biocompatible label for cellular imaging and showed an excellent quenching response by the addition of Pd2+ ions. As a result, the proposed CDs-based probe could be utilized in various environmental applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Natural source
Red Beetroot
Static quenching
Pd2+ ion

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs using the natural source
Selectivity and analytical efficiency of CDs probe for Pd2+ sensing
Measurement of Pd2+ in real water samples
Cytotoxicity and bioimaging application of CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Amirkabir University of Technology (Tehran, Iran).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a HR-TEM image of synthesized CDs, scale bar is 50 nm b, XRD spectrum of CDs, c Raman pattern of CDs d the Zeta potential of CDs at pH 7.0, and e) FT-IR spectrum.
a XPS survey spectrum of the prepared CDs and High-resolution spectrums of b C1s, c N1s, and d S2p of the XPS analysis of CDs.

a UV–vis absorbance, excitation, and emission spectra of CDs, b the effect of pH on the PL intensity of the synthesized CDs, and c emission intensity of CDs at various concentrations of NaCl (0–5 M), d PL spectra of CDs under 120 min continuous illumination at an excitation wavelength of 450 nm, and e fluorescence spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths varying from 300 to 540 nm.

a The selectivity of CDs (3 μg mL−1) for Pd2+ ions in the attendance of several metal ions at pH 7.0. the concentration of Pd2+ and other metal ions are 10 µM, b the PL response of CDs (3 μg mL−1) in the presence of different concentrations of Pd2+ ions (0–50 μM) and, c the Stern–Volmer plot of CDs after addition of various concentrations of Pd2+ ions (0–50 μM). Inset: a linear relationship of F0/F versus concentrations of Pd2+ ions in the range of 3–43 μM. F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities at 525 nm in the presence and absence of Pd2+ ions, respectively.

a The cell viability of Hela cells after treatment with CDs via the MTT method and the cellular imaging of Hela cells using a laser scanning confocal microscope in the bright field after incubation with b CDs, c CDs/Pd2+ ions at the excitation wavelength of 450 nm. Laser scanning confocal microscope images of Hela cells after incubation with d CDs, e CDs/Pd2+ ions. The standard deviation from three separate experiments was reported using error bars.
Comparing optical characteristics for prepared CDs in this work with reported works to detect Pd2+ ions.
Results for recoveries of Pd2+ ions in real water samples.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of photoluminescent carbon dots derived from red beetroot as a selective probe for Pd2+ detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Amirkabir University of Technology, 15875-4413 Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Amirkabir University of Technology, 15875-4413 Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, the development of green methods for carbon dots (CDs) synthesis using natural precursors has received considerable attention. In this regard, fluorescent CDs with high quantum yield have been synthesized from Red Beetroot as a natural precursor via a low-cost, simple, and green one-step hydrothermal treatment. The prepared CDs with a size range of 5–7 nm exhibited significant advantages, including great water solubility, low cytotoxicity, high quantum yield (27.6%), incredible resistance to pH alteration, high salty environments, and photobleaching. The fluorescence emission wavelength with maximum intensity for CDs was 525 nm at the excitation wavelength of 450 nm. These green-emitting CDs were employed for selective and sensitive measurement of Pd2+ in an aqueous solution. The addition of Pd2+ resulted in fluorescence quenching of CDs through a static quenching mechanism with an acceptable limit of detection of 33 nM in a linear range from 3 μM to 43 μM. Also, the proposed fluorescent probe can be applied as a suitable sensor for measuring Pd2+ ions in Mineral and Well water samples with satisfactory recovery rates from 96.6 to 105.0%. Generated fluorescent CDs have been used as an attractive biocompatible label for cellular imaging and showed an excellent quenching response by the addition of Pd2+ ions. As a result, the proposed CDs-based probe could be utilized in various environmental applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, polymeric, semiconductor, and carbon nanomaterials have received a great deal of consideration and played critical roles in numerous areas such as biosensors, bioimaging, and biomedicine [1]. Carbon dots (CDs), a comparatively new class of carbon nanomaterials, typically under 10 nm in size, were reported for the first time in 2004 [2]. CDs have attracted much attention because of their novel characteristics, including low toxicity, thermal stability, biocompatibility, superior photoluminescence, water-solubility, excellent quantum yield, and easy synthesis from a wide variety of carbon sources [3,4]. There are various methods for preparing CDs, such as microwave, microplasma, electrochemical, laser ablation, and hydrothermal, which may or may not involve difficult, precise, and costly preparation stages [5,6]. Among them, the hydrothermal method is the most widely accepted because of its simple and convenient instruments/operation, one-step synthetic process, high quantum yield, and low-cost reagents [7]. Recently, developing the routes of green synthesis of CDs using natural precursors has received considerable attention. Among the several natural substances that may be applied as precursors for the synthesis of CDs, food products are viable options as inexpensive and non-toxic sources for the preparation of highly soluble CDs in water [3,8]. Therefore, the wide variety of food products such as milk [9], saccharum officinarum [10], rose-heart radish [11], orange [12], green tea [13], sargassum fluitans [14], ocimum sanctum [15], pineapple [16], lemon and grapefruit [17], coffee beans [18], banana peels [19], manilkara zapota [20], and apple [21] have been studied for synthesis of fluorescent CDs by hydrothermal method. These natural precursors have been employed for different metal ions detection and bioimaging applications.

Developing very selective and sensitive probes for heavy and noble metal ions measurements is an active field of study as they are essential in the chemical, environmental, and biological sciences. Palladium is one of the rarest noble metals that is widely used in jewelry, dental and medical appliances, catalysts, cancer therapy, fuel cells, electronics, medicine, and many more [22]. However, palladium pollution requires further purification due to palladium residue and compounds left in drugs, food, and water. Thus, the detection of palladium ions (Pd2+) is of remarkable attention because Pd2+ ions can cause major noxious effects on people’s health, such as severe eye and skin irritations. These effects happen due to the formation of complexes among palladium and a few bio-macromolecules like RNA, DNA, and proteins. As a result, the maximum level for palladium in pharmaceuticals has been restricted to 5–10 ppm, and the most threshold dietary intake is limited to 1.5–15 µg day−1 per person [23].

Recently, fluorescent-based sensors for detecting noble metals such as Pd2+ ions have been the most popular because of the simplicity, rapidity, and ease of the experimental analysis. For example, Gao et al. reported the synthesis of red-emission CDs by a hydrothermal method that can provide a suitable probe for bioimaging applications and detecting Pd2+ with a quantum yield of 12.1% [24]. Dong et al. synthesized a fluorescent sensing probe using Momordica charantia to detect Pd2+ ions with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.348 μM [25]. Sharma et al. designed carbon-based nanomaterials as a “Turn off” probe to detect noble metal Pd2+ ions by quenching the CDs [26]. Pawar et al. have demonstrated the application of red-emitting CDs for sensing Pd2+ in aqueous solutions with LOD of 60 nM [27]. Most of the precursors from previous reports detect Pd2+ via toxic chemicals formation and low selectivity. Consequently, design and preparation of fast response, selective, and sensitive fluorescent probes using natural sources to detect Pd2+ remains as an essential and challenging task.

This work reports a low-cost, high efficient, simple and green synthesis of fluorescent CDs from Red Beetroot as a natural precursor through the one-step hydrothermal treatment. Red Beetroot is one of the several cultivated types of Beta vulgaris produced on a large scale and cultivated in different weather conditions. Red Beetroots contain various active chemicals, including oxalic acids, α-lipoic acid, phenolic acids, betalains, proteins, carbohydrates, and sugars [28,29], which could help synthesizing CDs for a selective and sensitive detection of Pd2+ ions. A considerable advantage of the prepared CDs is its green synthesis as well as the presence of N and S heteroatoms. Unlike other compounds, the aforementioned synthesis CDs does not require severe synthetic conditions, complex post-treatment methods, strong solvents, and surface passivation reagents. Besides, the proposed CDs showed low cytotoxicity, high quantum yield, excellent water solubility, and superior photostability. It was found that synthesized CDs in this work exhibited a sensitive and selective response for measuring Pd2+ ions in an aqueous solution in the presence of 15 other metal ions. Furthermore, these CDs could be successfully applied as fluorescent sensing probes to detect Pd2+ ions with an acceptable LOD of 33 nM. As far as we know, the CDs prepared using Red Beetroot to measure Pd2+ ions have not been reported up to now. Table S1 compares the physicochemical properties of several previously prepared green CDs with the present work.

The Red Beetroot was prepared from a local supermarket (Tehran, Iran). All metal ions (Ag+, As3+,Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Pd2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Se2-, Ni2+, Rh3+, Pt2+, Zn2+) and Rhodamine 6G as a fluorescence standard reference (98%) were obtained from Merck. Other chemical analytical grade reagents and solutions were utilized without additional treatment. The dialysis membrane (MWCO of 3500 Da) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Deionized water was applied in all analyses.

Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were conducted using Perkin Elmer LS55 fluorescence spectrometer in quartz cuvette cells. UV–vis spectroscopy was performed by a Perkin Elmer Lambda 45 spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was taken with a Bruker alpha electron spectrometer and KBr as a standard. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was measured with a Radiation: 1.5406 Cu, Generator: 40KV, 40 mA, Imax = 1529. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was obtained with a JEM-2010 FEF transmission electron microscope. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) image was taken with a FEI TEC9G 20 operating at 200 kV. Fluorescence lifetime was taken using an Edinburgh FLS920 with a xenon lamp. Zeta potential was obtained from a Cordouan Tech (WALLIS). Raman spectrum was carried out with Takram 785, Teksan microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed by a BESTEC photoelectron spectrometer with Al Kα = 1486.6 eV as an X-ray excitation source and a vacuum system of 10-10 mbar.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs using the natural source
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were prepared through a simple hydrothermal method using Red Beetroot as a natural precursor. In brief, 10 g of Red Beetroot was chopped into small pieces, mixed with 70 mL of deionized water, stirred for 20 min, and sonicated for 1 h in an ultrasonic bath. Afterward, the mixture was transferred into a 200 mL Teflon-lined laboratory autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 10 h in an oven. Then, the suspension was centrifuged (10000 rpm, 30 min) to remove large unreacted particles and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to collect the CDs solution. Finally, the resulting solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (3.5 kDa) for three days to remove impurities and diluted for later usage [30].

The quenching effect of Pd2+ ions on PL intensity of CDs was measured at ambient temperature as follows. In a typical test, 10 µL of CDs solution (0.3 mg mL−1) was added into 1 mL phosphate-buffered saline (10 mmol L-1) (PBS, pH = 7). Different concentrations of Pd2+ ions varying from 0 to 50 µmol L-1 were added to the solutions containing CDs, and the fluorescent spectrums of CDs were measured after 3 min at the excitation wavelength of 450 nm. Similarly, the selectivity of Pd2+ detection was confirmed with the addition of other metal ions instead of Pd2+. All the emission spectrums were achieved at the PL wavelength of 525 nm. The selectivity and sensitivity tests were performed three times.

To determine the proper performance of CDs in the real samples, Well water was collected from south of Tehran, Iran, and the Mineral water was supplied from a local supermarket (Tehran, Iran). The Pd2+ ions were measured using the standard addition method in real samples. All samples were filtered into microporous membranes (0.22 µM) to gather colloid particles. Then CDs were added to 1 mL of each real water sample (pH = 7) to obtain a 3 µg mL−1 concentration, and the samples were subsequently enriched with various concentrations of Pd2+ ions. Finally, the emission intensity of samples was recorded at the excitation wavelength 450 nm using the approach described above.

Cell cytotoxicity experiment of prepared CDs was performed using MTT assay. Firstly, the Hela cells were grown within a DMEM medium with 1% penicillin–streptomycin and 10% FBS under CO2 5% in an incubator into the cell culture plate at 37 ℃ over 24 h. Afterward, the cells were replaced by DMEM containing different concentrations of CDs (0–1000 μg mL−1) and incubated at 37 ℃ with CO2 5% for 24 h. Then, the cells were washed with PBS buffer (20 mL, pH 7.4), and after the addition of MTT salt solution, each cell was incubated for 5 h (3 mg mL−1 in PBS). After aspirating the culture medium by MTT solution, 150 µL of dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) was injected into wells and shaken at ambient conditions over 5 min. Finally, the mixture’s absorption was measured with a microplate reader (ELx800, Biotek, USA) at 450 nm. The control experiments were carried out with non-treated cells by CDs under the conditions mentioned [31,32], and all experiments were conducted three times

The Hela cells were cultured in a DMEM medium at 37 °C regularly containing 5% CO2 and10% FBS. The cells were incubated with 1 mg mL−1 of CDs for 3 h and 1 mg mL−1 CDs/100 µM Pd2+ ions for 30 min. After that, Hela cells were washed three times by phosphate buffer to eliminate un-reacted CDs. Ultimately, the treated cells were preserved in PBS for cell imaging study by a confocal laser scanning microscope (Leica TCS SPE, CLSM, Tehran, Iran).

The hydrothermal treatment of Red Beetroot at 180 °C for 10 h produced CDs nanoparticles. The morphology of the resulting CDs was characterized using TEM images. As illustrated in (Fig. S1), the CDs had uniform dispersion in water with no apparent aggregation, spherical shapes, and a narrow size around 5–7 nm in diameter [11,33]. To further study the morphology and particle size of the CDs, an HR-TEM image was taken. As seen from (Fig. 1 a), the prepared CDs had nearly spherical shapes and an average diameter around 5–7 nm with no apparent aggregation. As demonstrated in (Fig. 1b), the XRD spectrum of the CDs displayed a broad diffraction peak at approximately 2θ = 22°A assigned to the plane (002) and amorphous nature of carbon atoms which were packed by alkyl chains in CDs. The amorphous and imperfect crystalline nature of CDs is also ascribed to the higher number of functional groups at the surface of CDs [34,35]. The Raman spectrum was measured for distinction among sp2-hybridized and sp3-hybridized carbon atoms. As illustrated in (Fig. 1c), two wide bands at about 1358 and 1566 cm−1 detected in the Raman pattern of CDs were assigned to the existence of sp3- hybridized (D band) and sp2- hybridized (G band) carbon atoms, respectively. The intensity ratio for disordered D-band and crystalline G-band was about 0.91, exhibiting that the CDs had amorphous graphitized nature [36]. The Zeta potential measurement showed that the CDs surface is negatively charged, with a value of −6.68 mV (Fig. 1d), because of water-soluble carboxylic and hydroxyl groups distribution at the surface of CDs. Also, these hydrophilic groups on the surface of CDs can provide many emissive traps that are very significant for the high PL properties of CDs [37].

Additionally, FT-IR spectroscopy was utilized to show functional groups alternation on the surface of CDs. As seen from (Fig. 1e), the peaks centered at 1628 and 1750 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of amide and carboxylic C = O bonds, respectively. The sharp peak at 3422 cm−1 corresponded to the O–H and N–H stretching vibrations. The bands indicating the C-O, C = C, C-N, and C = N stretching vibrations, were located at about 1202, 1560, 1457, and 1654 cm−1, respectively [38]. Moreover, the bands at 2853 and 2923 cm−1 were indicated the C–H vibrations, confirming non-graphitic chains at the surface of CDs. Also, the peaks of C–H bending and C-S stretching vibrations were revealed at around 650–825 and 1115 cm−1, respectively. Finally, the characteristic peak at 1385 cm−1 was demonstrated the stretching vibrations of C–C bonds [34]. As a result, the FT-IR spectrum illustrated the presence of carboxyl, carbonyl, and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs, providing high water solubility and stability in the environmental solutions.

XPS analysis was done to get more information about the functional groups on the surface of CDs. As illustrated in (Fig. 2 a), the survey spectrum XPS of the prepared CDs indicated four peaks at 283.5, 397.5, 530.6, and 166 eV, assigned to C1s, N1s, O1s, and S2p, respectively. As demonstrated in (Fig. 2b), the high-resolved spectrum of C1s consisted of five peaks at 284.6, 285.2, 286.1, 286.3, and 288.1, which were assigned to C–C/C = C, C-S, C-N, C-O, and C = N/C = O bonds, respectively [39]. The high-resolution spectrum of N1s can be deconvoluted into three peaks centered at 401.3, 400.4, and 399.6 confirming, the presence of graphitic, pyrrolic, and pyridinic nitrogen groups in the structure of prepared CDs, respectively (Fig. 2c) [40]. The S2p displayed two peaks at 163.9, 169.4, representing the -C-S- and –C-SOX- groups in CDs (Fig. 2d). The -C-S- band can be taken apart into two peaks ascribing to S2p1/2 (163.7) and S2p3/2 (164.6) due to their spin–orbit couplings [41]. The latter can be fitted in three peaks, including 167.6 (-C-SO2-), 168.6 (-C-SO3-), and 169.2 (-C-SO4-) [42]. The XPS analytical data also showed that these CDs were mostly composed of elements including C (76.84%), O (15.99%), N (4.59%), and S (2.59). Finally, the data obtained from XPS analysis were in agreement with the results from FT-IR.

The optical properties of the CDs, including UV–vis absorption, emission spectra, and stability, were investigated. As indicated in (Fig. 3 a), the UV–vis spectrum of CDs exhibited two typical absorption peaks at around 227 and 293 nm relevant to the π-π* transition of aromatic C = C bonds and n-π* transition of C = O bonds respectively [43]. These results confirm the aromatic system in CDs that causes strong fluorescence peaks due to excited defect surface states [44]. It was also shown in (Fig. 3a) that the optimal excitation and emission spectrums of synthesized CDs were conducted at wavelengths 450 nm and 535 nm, respectively.

Again, to deliberate the resistance of fluorescent CDs, the effect of pH, ionic strength, and time on the emissions intensity of synthesized CDs was measured. As demonstrated in (Fig. 3b), as the pH values increased from 1.0 to 7.0, the emission intensity of CDs increased. Then, simultaneously with increasing the pH to values higher than 8.0, the emission intensity of CDs decreased. The optimum pH value to achieve the high emission intensity of CDs was obtained at pH 7.0. Most of the natural CDs are self-passivated due to various functional groups by virtue of carbohydrates and proteins in the structure of precursors. The hetero-atom doping achieved by self-passivation offered an exquisite impact on the optical properties leading to well-versed applications in the bio-medical field. The strong response of the CDs to pH alteration was considered to be derived from the protonation–deprotonation of the amino and carboxyl groups on their surface. Since pH variations could enforce protonation or deprotonation of emissive sites in the CDs cores, the emission intensity of the CDs in acidic and alkaline conditions can be significantly decreased [45–47]. It showed that the prepared CDs would be stable in the most physiological conditions and suitable for cellular and bioimaging applications [34]. The ionic strength effect on the PL intensity of CDs was measured at various concentrations of NaCl solution (0–5 M) (Fig. 3c). The emission intensity remained almost constant with the increase in NaCl solution concentration, displaying the high stability of CDs in concentrated salt environments for practical applications [36]. After a 60-day storage period under ambient conditions (Fig. S2), the PL spectra of CDs and 120 min continuous illumination at an excitation wavelength of 450 nm (Fig. 3d) indicate no remarkable change in emission intensity and confirm the excellent photostability of CDs.

To further study the PL spectra of CDs, the emission peaks of CDs were measured at different excitation wavelengths varying from 300 to 540 nm. As displayed in (Fig. 3e), the PL intensity gradually increased when the CDs were excited from 300 to 450 nm then decreased by changing the excitation wavelength from 460 to 540 nm. The PL spectrums were obviously red-shifted from 525 nm to 558 nm when the excitation wavelength ranged from 450 nm to 540 nm. It happens due to the various sizes of CDs, different surface states, and distribution of various organic groups on the surface of CDs. An excitation spectrum showed the wavelengths of light that a sample would absorb to emit at a specified wavelength. No strong emission spectrum is observed at short wavelengths (227 nm or 293 nm). However, according to the references in Table S2, it is common for CDs to have different absorption and excitation wavelengths. Similar to the standard CDs, the prepared CDs exhibited excitation-dependent emission character. These behaviors resulted from the different sizes of CDs and different surface states due to the different organic groups on the surface of CDs [11,48–51]. The surface state of CDs could affect the band gap, thus creating different emission characteristics [49]. With the increase of functional groups (C-OH, C-O-C, NH2) on the surface of CDs, a large number of epoxide or hydroxy corresponding localized electronic states are expected to form under the π* state. These functional groups might change the electronic structures, resulting in extra new energy levels between n-π* gaps. Finally, many types of radiative recombination could happen that create a wide range of excitation energies and excitation-wavelength-dependent emission [50,51]. These results demonstrated that the emission peaks depend on the excitation wavelengths and defined the optimum emission peak at 525 nm with the excitation wavelength of 450 nm [52].

The quantum yield (QY) of the as-synthesized CDs was experimentally calculated using Rhodamine 6G in water with the known QY of 0.95 as a reference based on the equation (1) [53,54]:
(1)
QY
S
=
QY
R
(
A R
A S
)
(
I S
I R
)
(
N
S
2
N
R
2
)
where R and S denote the standard reference and CDs samples, respectively. N refers to the solvent’s refractive index, I displays integrated photoluminescence spectra, A is the absorbance, and QY is quantum yield. Based on (Fig. S3), it was found that the photoluminescence QY of CDs in an aqueous solution was 27.6% at an excitation wavelength of 450 nm. The high QY values displayed that the prepared CDs have desirable fluorescent properties. The absorbance of samples was kept below 0.06 at the excitation wavelength of 450 nm. No fluorescence emission intensity was observed at short excitation wavelengths like 293 nm and 227 nm absorption bands for these CDs. Based on previous studies [32,48], it is possible that the excitation and absorption wavelengths are not the same, so the QY was performed at the best excitation wavelength (450 nm), and the absorption values were read at the absorption wavelength. The detailed results are presented in Table S3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and analytical efficiency of CDs probe for Pd2+ sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Detection of noble metal ions such as Pd2+ is crucial owing to toxicity, noxious effects on people’s health, and expensiveness. In order to determine the selectivity of the CDs-based probe, the fluorescence response of CDs was studied in the presence of 15 metal ions (Ag+, Al3+, As3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Rh3+, Pb2+, Pd2+, Pt2+, Se2-, Zn2+) with a concentration of 10 µM and 10 typical anions (NO3 –, NO2 –, F-, Cl-, I-, S2-, CN–, SO4 2-, SO3 -, CO3 2–) with a concentration of 10 µM under optimized conditions. As presented in (Fig. 4 a) ((F0/F)-1 versus metal ions), and (Fig. S4) (F /F0 versus anions), the fluorescent probe exhibited high selectivity towards the Pd2+ ions compared to the other metal ions and typical anions that had no noticeable effect on the fluorescence quenching of the prepared probe. To survey the successful synthesis of green-emitting CDs and their quenching response in the presence of Pd2+ ions, fluorescence microscopy photos were taken before and after the addition of Pd2+ ions. As shown in (Fig. S5a,b), after adding Pd2+ ions, the green-emitting CDs were quenched by the Pd2+ ions, and the emission intensity was reduced. To further observe the green emission of CDs and their response to Pd2+ ions, CDs were photographed under UV light before and after the addition of Pd2+ ions. The aqueous solution of CDs showed a strong green emission under UV light, while the fluorescence of CDs was quenched after adding Pd2+ ions (Fig. S5c-d).

In order to achieve the optimal conditions for detecting Pd2+ ions by CDs, the reaction time of CDs to Pd2+ ions was investigated and optimized. As shown in (Fig. S6), the CDs emission intensity decreased immediately after adding a solution containing 10 μM of Pd2+ ions. Therefore, 3 min as the optimal reaction time of CDs to Pd2+ ions was selected for further studies. The short response time of CDs to Pd2+ ions makes it possible to use them to detect Pd2+ ions rapidly. Furthermore, the quantitative measurement of Pd2+ ions was carried out under optimum conditions. As indicated in (Fig. 4b), the PL spectra of CDs were measured after adding various concentrations of Pd2+ ions (0–50 μM). They showed that the emission intensity of CDs progressively decreased by the increase in the amount of Pd2+ ions. The calibration curve (F0/F versus ion concentration) was plotted and exhibited an excellent linear relationship (R2 = 0.9978) with concentrations of Pd2+ ions between 3 and 43 μM (Fig. 4c). The linear equation was calculated to measure the LOD of the CDs-based probe:
(2)
Fo
F
= 0.149 X + 0.9267

F0 and F are the emission intensity of CDs in the absence and the presence of Pd2+ ions at the excitation wavelength 450 nm, respectively, and X represents the concentration of Pd2+ ions. The LOD was determined according to equation (3)
δ /m ( δ is the standard deviation of blank and m is the calibration curve slope) [52]. Based on the calibration curve slope (0.149) and standard deviation of blank (0.001639), the LOD was 33 nM, indicating an acceptable amount compared to previous CDs-based probes for detecting Pd2+ ions reported in Table 1 . These results showed that the constructed probe has sufficient selectivity and sensitivity to be nominated as a sensor for Pd2+ detection.

Previous sections proved that the Pd2+ ions could significantly quench the synthesized CDs as a fluorescent probe. Typically, two types of mechanism, including dynamic and static quenching, are defined for the quenching behavior of CDs through the quencher and fluorophore. Dynamic quenching occurs because of collisions between fluorophore and quencher and in which the fluorescence quenching is dependent on time and concentration. However, static quenching with a linear dependence quenching happens due to forming a ground-state complex by the interaction through the functional groups of fluorophore and quencher [55]. Various tests, including UV–vis absorption, Zeta potential, and fluorescence lifetime, have been performed to investigate the possible quenching mechanism of prepared CDs by the Pd2+ ions. Fluorescence lifetime evaluation generally can be used to determine the type of possible mechanism between CDs and quenchers. In this work, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs was performed in the attendance and absence of Pd2+ ions, and the results were shown in (Fig. S7a). The lifetime for CDs before and after the addition of Pd2+ ions was calculated to be 4.47 ns and 4.33 ns, respectively. As seen from (Fig. S7a), the fluorescence lifetime of CDs did not change remarkably, affirming the efficiency of coordination or chelation interactions between Pd2+ ions and various functional groups on the surface of CDs and fluorescence quenching through a static quenching mechanism. To deliberate the quenching mechanism of CDs, the Stern-Volmer equation was investigated at ambient conditions [56]:
(3)
Fo
F
= 1 + K s v
Q

K SV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant, F0 and F are the PL intensity of CDs in the absence and presence of Pd2+ ions, and [Q] is the quencher concentration (Pd2+). The bimolecular quenching constant (Kq ) is calculated based on the slope of the Stern-Volmer curve (Ksv = 14.9 × 104 M−1) and the equation Kq = Ksv/T0 where T0 is the fluorescence lifetime of CDs (T0 = 4.47 ns) in the absence of Pd2+ ions. The relatively large value of 3.33 × 1013 M−1s−1 for Kq according to the maximum amount for dynamic quenching (2 × 1010 M−1s−1) represents a static mechanism and the formation of the ground-state complex that comes from the interaction between functional groups on the surface of CDs and Pd2+ ions [26,57].

Moreover, the UV–vis absorption spectrums of CDs before and after adding Pd2+ ions were studied (Fig. S7b), demonstrating that the absorbance of bands centered at 227 nm and 293 nm was increased, which might affirm the formation of a stable non-fluorescence complex between Pd2+ ions and functional groups on the surface of CDs [25,57,58]. To further evaluate the quenching mechanism of the prepared CDs, the Zeta potential analysis of CDs was done in the presence and absence of Pd2+ ions. As exhibited in (Fig. S7c), with the addition of Pd2+ ions, the Zeta potential values were negatively charged from − 6.68 mV to –23.64 mV. Negative charge values of CDs after the addition of Pd2+ ions represented the coordination of the oxygen or amino groups on the surface of the CDs with Pd2+ ions and the formation of the ground-state complex, so the positive charge of CDs was protected with Pd2+ ions [59]. The amine groups on the surface of CDs are protonated, and the transfer of protons from amino groups to the conjugated structure of CDs improves the fluorescence properties [60,61]. The presence of Pd2+ ions can prevent the protonation of the amine group on the surface of CDs; therefore, the zeta potential values became more negative, and the fluorescence properties were reduced [62]. Consequently, it can be concluded that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the obtained CDs was based on the formation of the ground-state complex and static quenching mechanism between CDs and Pd2+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of Pd2+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the efficiency of fluorescent CDs in real samples, an additional standard method was carried out on the Mineral and Well water samples spiked with different concentrations of Pd2+ ions (0–40 µM). In this method, standard concentrations of Pd2+ ions were added into real samples, then the recovery rate is calculated by the emission intensity of CDs. Based on the results given in Table 2 , the acceptable recovery rates for all samples ranged from 96.6 to 105.0%, with the RSD in the range of 1.74–4.91%, suggesting that the proposed fluorescent probe can be a reliable and high-performance choice for measuring Pd2+ ions in real samples [63,64].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and bioimaging application of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the excellent optical properties of the CDs, such as high biocompatibility, superior photoluminescence, and excellent quantum yield, the CDs-based probe was recommended as a candidate for bioimaging applications. The cytotoxicity of CDs was assessed in Hela cells using the MTT method. The Cells were treated with various concentrations of CDs (0–1000 μg mL−1) for 24 h. As demonstrated in (Fig. 5 a), CDs exhibited no considerable cytotoxicity to the Hela cells, even after 24 h treatment at a high concentration of 1000 µg mL−1. As a result, these non-toxic CDs can be suggested as an attractive biocompatible label for bio-applications [65].

Furthermore, the cellular imaging of CDs was performed using a laser scanning confocal microscope. The Hela cells were lightened after labeling with CDs, clarifying the significant cellular uptake of CDs by the cells. As illustrated in (Fig. 5b-e), the cells were imaged through a confocal microscope, demonstrating that the CDs can easily enter cytosols and parts with high concentrations of fluorescent nanoparticles due to the presence of different functional groups on their surface and hydrophilic nature. Also, CDs can move through cell membranes, and no changes were found in their fluorescence properties. The morphology of Hela cells stayed almost unchanged after incubation with fluorescent CDs and CDs/Pd2+ ions, and no cell damage was found. Consequently, these results were confirmed the extremely low toxicity of the prepared CDs from Red Beetroot for bioimaging, providing a good alternative for light-sensitive organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots with known biotoxicity. They could also be used as a sensitive nanosensor for measuring Pd2+ ions and a bio-labeling agent for cell imaging [66].

In summary, low-cost, high quantum yield and green CDs were obtained from Red Beetroot as a natural precursor through the one-step hydrothermal treatment. Prepared CDs with a size of 5–7 nm exhibited excellent stability under various conditions, including good resistance to pH, high salty environments, and photobleaching. Green emission CDs showed a sensitive and selective response to detect noble metal ions of Pd2+ in an aqueous solution in the presence of 15 other metal ions. Furthermore, these CDs could be successfully applied as fluorescent sensing probes to detect Pd2+ ions with an acceptable LOD of 33 nM in a linear range from 3 to 43 μM. Also, non-toxic CDs can be used a promising biocompatible label for cell imaging and bio-related applications. The proposed fluorescent probe could be applied as a reliable and high-performance sensor for measuring Pd2+ ions in real water samples with an acceptable recovery rate of 96.6–105.40%.

Nazanin Hashemi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Mohammad H. Mousazadeh: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Amirkabir University of Technology (Tehran, Iran).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2021.113534.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
